primary_variables.matched_definition: Exposure involving active infection by virus, bacteria, fungus, or parasite.
evidence: we integrate a subcellular spatial dimension with TPCA and use this method to functionally characterize a virus-induced biomolecular condensate in the nucleus.
interrogate nuclear-cytoplasmic shuttling events and intranuclear movement of host and viral proteins throughout infection of primary human fibroblasts with a mutant strain of HSV-1
infection of primary human fibroblasts with a mutant strain of HSV-1
we chose to use the ICP0-RF strain of HSV-1
We opted to use a relatively high multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 5 for this experiment

monitoring localization markers following thermal denaturation and nuclear/cytoplasmic fractionation shows that these markers retain their separation and enrichment in their corresponding subcellular compartment across denaturation temperatures and HSV-1 infection time points [3, 8, and 15 hours post infection (hpi)]
In this study, 0 hpi refers to the moment at which inoculum is introduced to the monolayer.

replicate_hints: a single PCR strip representing all five temperatures of a single replicate of a single condition
multiplex_channel_hints: Nuclear and cytoplasmic temperatures of the same condition and replicate were multiplexed together (126–131 N, five cytoplasmic fraction temperatures, five nuclear fraction temperatures), along with a reference sample (131C).

evidence: Spatially resolving the dynamic behavior of protein interactomes throughout HSV-1 infection
we performed a spatiotemporal interactome study across the replication cycle of the prominent human pathogen, HSV-1

base_candidates_ranked.reason: The methods explicitly describe TMT 11-plex labeling and the use of a Q Exactive HF instrument with a data-dependent MS2 method for the quantitative mixture.
The text mentions that subcellular fractionation without prior thermal denaturation was validated using label-free data-independent acquisition (DIA) MS.

modifier_candidates_ranked.reason: The experiment is a Thermal Proximity Coaggregation (TPCA) analysis, which is a variant of thermal proteome profiling, involving denaturation at five temperatures (37° to 55.3°C) to monitor protein solubility and PPIs.
The study integrates nuclear/cytoplasmic fractionation (N/C-TPCA) to resolve spatial PPI networks.
